patch_instructions: Patch 1 — detail_evidence (ISS-1847, single insertion). In data/wins.json, locate WIN id="017". Find the existing sentence: "all methods agree, ruling out any global systematic that would affect geometric parallax alone." Insert the new sentence in  (below) IMMEDIATELY AFTER that sentence (preserving the leading space) and IMMEDIATELY BEFORE the existing "Spacecraft-based measurements provide even stronger evidence:" sentence. The full replacement_detail_evidence string in this file is the result of doing exactly that — the decider may either apply the insertion as a targeted edit OR overwrite the entire field with replacement_detail_evidence; both produce the same result.
No other WIN-017 fields are modified. No other WINs are modified.

current_field_values.detail_evidence_first_chars: Gaia Collaboration et al. (2023, A&A 674, A1) …
current_field_values.detail_evidence_last_chars: …than the firmament height; no dome-embedded star could be observed, let alone measured with distance-dependent parallax.

insertion_anchor_before: all methods agree, ruling out any global systematic that would affect geometric parallax alone.
insertion_anchor_after: Spacecraft-based measurements provide even stronger evidence:
inserted_sentence: The cleanest independence test is the raw Gaia astrometric residuals — the annual sinusoidal position signature has amplitude that correlates with spectroscopic-class distance estimates (which set absolute magnitudes from spectral classification rather than from a parallax-calibrated period-luminosity relation), confirming that the 1/d pattern is in the geometry of the data and not an artifact of the photometric calibration chain.
current_detail_evidence: Gaia Collaboration et al. (2023, A&A 674, A1) provides parallax measurements for 1.8 billion stars. Parallax is inversely proportional to distance: Proxima Centauri (4.24 ly) shows 0.768 arcsec, Sirius (8.6 ly) shows 0.379 arcsec — a 2× distance difference producing a 2× parallax difference. A rigid firmament wobble would produce one fixed angular displacement for all objects: at firmament height H = 9,086 km, a 20 m amplitude gives arctan(20 / 9,086,000) ≈ 0.454 arcsec regardless of target distance. Invoking a non-rigid or variable-amplitude wobble to mimic the 1/distance variation introduces an unfalsifiable free parameter. Gaia DR3 also cross-validates parallax using independent photometric distance methods (Cepheids, RR Lyrae, spectrophotometry) — all methods agree, ruling out any global systematic that would affect geometric parallax alone. Spacecraft-based measurements provide even stronger evidence: NASA's New Horizons probe, located 7 billion km from Earth in April 2020, measured parallax shifts of 32.4 arcsec (Proxima Centauri) and 15.7 arcsec (Wolf 359) that scale precisely with each star's distance (Throop et al. 2020, Research Notes AAS 4, 74). Under the dome model, New Horizons would be ~770,000× further than the firmament height; no dome-embedded star could be observed, let alone measured with distance-dependent parallax.
replacement_detail_evidence: Gaia Collaboration et al. (2023, A&A 674, A1) provides parallax measurements for 1.8 billion stars. Parallax is inversely proportional to distance: Proxima Centauri (4.24 ly) shows 0.768 arcsec, Sirius (8.6 ly) shows 0.379 arcsec — a 2× distance difference producing a 2× parallax difference. A rigid firmament wobble would produce one fixed angular displacement for all objects: at firmament height H = 9,086 km, a 20 m amplitude gives arctan(20 / 9,086,000) ≈ 0.454 arcsec regardless of target distance. Invoking a non-rigid or variable-amplitude wobble to mimic the 1/distance variation introduces an unfalsifiable free parameter. Gaia DR3 also cross-validates parallax using independent photometric distance methods (Cepheids, RR Lyrae, spectrophotometry) — all methods agree, ruling out any global systematic that would affect geometric parallax alone. The cleanest independence test is the raw Gaia astrometric residuals — the annual sinusoidal position signature has amplitude that correlates with spectroscopic-class distance estimates (which set absolute magnitudes from spectral classification rather than from a parallax-calibrated period-luminosity relation), confirming that the 1/d pattern is in the geometry of the data and not an artifact of the photometric calibration chain. Spacecraft-based measurements provide even stronger evidence: NASA's New Horizons probe, located 7 billion km from Earth in April 2020, measured parallax shifts of 32.4 arcsec (Proxima Centauri) and 15.7 arcsec (Wolf 359) that scale precisely with each star's distance (Throop et al. 2020, Research Notes AAS 4, 74). Under the dome model, New Horizons would be ~770,000× further than the firmament height; no dome-embedded star could be observed, let alone measured with distance-dependent parallax.
holes_addressed: WIN-017.c3 hole #3 (moderate) — "CROSS-VALIDATION CIRCULARITY PREEMPTION STILL MISSING (c2 minor #2 carry-forward)." The c2 review and c3 review both flagged that current text leans on Cepheid+RR-Lyrae+spectrophotometry agreement to dismiss systematics, but a sophisticated dome defender can correctly note that Cepheid and RR Lyrae period-luminosity relations are CALIBRATED using parallaxes of nearby standard candles. Hole closed by inserting one sentence pointing readers to spectroscopic-class distance estimates as an independence-axis cross-check (spectral classification → absolute magnitude → distance does not require period-luminosity calibration against parallax).
new_evidence_added: Conceptual reference to spectroscopic-class distance estimates (a.k.a. spectroscopic parallax) as an independence axis distinct from period-luminosity-calibrated standard candles. No new external citation added; the appeal is to a well-known method described in any introductory stellar-astrophysics text and already implicit in "spectrophotometry" in the prior sentence — the insertion makes the independence-from-period-luminosity property explicit.

anticipated_objections.objection: "Spectroscopic parallax (spectral type + apparent magnitude → distance) still requires a luminosity calibration, and that calibration uses nearby Hipparcos / Gaia parallaxes for zero-pointing. So spectroscopic distances ARE calibrated against parallax — your independence claim collapses."
anticipated_objections.response: The objection conflates absolute-magnitude zero-pointing with distance scaling. Spectroscopic-class distances scale with apparent magnitude via a fixed M_V derived from spectral type — that scaling does not depend on parallax. Parallax only fixes the additive zero-point of the M_V table (and even that table is also constrained by main-sequence fitting in open clusters via the moving-cluster method, which is parallax-independent). The 1/d distance dependence — the geometric quantity at issue here — emerges from spectroscopic distances independently of how their absolute-magnitude zero-point is set. By contrast, the dome-defender circularity worry is about the slope/shape of the distance-parallax relation; that slope does not flow through any parallax-calibrated step in spectroscopic-class distance estimation.

anticipated_objections.objection: "Most of the 1.8 billion Gaia DR3 stars are within a few kpc and have direct geometric parallax measurements — the distances do not flow through Cepheid period-luminosity calibration at all. So why is preempting that circularity even necessary?"
anticipated_objections.response: Correct in fact, and this is precisely why the circularity worry is not load-bearing for the verdict — the New Horizons distance-dependent parallax measurement (770,000× the firmament height, with shifts that scale with each star's distance) is alone fatal to the wobble model regardless of any cross-method agreement. The inserted sentence is a defender-objection close-out, not an evidence pillar; we add it to remove a free attack surface, not because the verdict depends on it.

anticipated_objections.objection: "You added a sentence about 'spectroscopic-class distance estimates' but did not cite a specific paper or compilation that performs the spectroscopic-class-vs-Gaia-parallax correlation. Without that, your appeal to spectroscopic parallax is hand-wavy."
anticipated_objections.response: Granted that we have not pinned a single correlation paper here. The point of the inserted sentence is structural — to flag for readers that an independence axis exists that does not flow through period-luminosity calibration. A future expansion can replace the conceptual appeal with a numerical correlation (e.g., a citation to the Geneva–Copenhagen survey of FGK stars where Strömgren-photometry distances cross-validate Hipparcos / Gaia parallaxes for ~14,000 nearby stars), but doing so requires more than the current single-sentence brief and is not load-bearing for the verdict. The minimal close-out preserves the structural argument without overcommitting on a specific dataset.

kernel_of_truth: A sophisticated dome defender is genuinely correct that period-luminosity relations for Cepheids and RR Lyrae are calibrated, in part, using parallaxes of nearby standard candles — most prominently the Riess et al. Gaia DR3 zero-point work that pins the absolute magnitude of ~75 Galactic Cepheids using Gaia parallaxes. So when our prior text said "all methods agree" without separating the methods by independence, it overstated the cleanness of the cross-check for those two methods specifically. The fix is not to retract "all methods agree" — they do agree — but to acknowledge which method on the list is independent of period-luminosity calibration, namely spectroscopic-class distances. This preserves the original argument while removing the legitimate piece of the dome-defender objection.
integration_notes: Decider: apply this as a single targeted detail_evidence edit on WIN-017. Either (a) replace the full detail_evidence field with replacement_detail_evidence, or (b) do an in-place insertion at insertion_anchor_before. Both produce identical output. After the edit, run tests/run.sh and verify WIN-017 still passes; the change is prose-only and does not affect any data values, counts, or classifications. ISS-1847 can be closed with this patch. The remaining open c3 holes (#2 monitoring tag, #4 New Horizons projection-factor caveat, #6 tldr_verdict referent polish) are NOT addressed here — they are tracked under their own ISS entries.
